Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/80885363|神酒]]"
revision.text:
[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|三方に乗せ献供された神酒]] '''神酒'''（みき、しんしゅ）とは、日本の[[Shinto|神道]]において[[Kami|神]]に供える[[Alcoholic drink|酒]]。
== 概要 == 「みき」という言葉は「酒」に「御」（み）をつけたもので、酒の美称である。通常はさらに「御」をつけて「おみき」という。『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』には「くし」の語があり、[[Okinawa Prefecture|沖縄県]]には「ウグス」の語がある。これらは「奇（くし）」に繋がるもので、酒の効能が奇瑞とされたことによるものである。
[[Shinsen|神饌]]には欠かせないもので、祭礼において、神酒（[[Sake|日本酒]]が使われる事が多い）を神前に供え、祭礼の終了後直会で神酒を戴く。神に供えられ霊が宿った酒を頂く、また他の神饌と同様の神と同じものを飲食するという意味がある。また、2年参りで礼拝をしたあとに、神酒を振る舞われる神社もある。
また、造り酒屋によっては、新酒を[[Kamidana|神棚]]に供えるところもある。
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.
『[[Engishiki|延喜式]]』によれば、白酒は神田で採れた米で醸造した酒をそのまま濾したもの、黒酒は白酒に[[Clerodendrum trichotomum|常山木]]の根の焼灰を加えて黒く着色した酒（灰持酒）であると記載されている。後にこれに倣って[[Amazake|醴]]を、ゴマの肝臓機能強化を知ってか悪酔い止めにと黒[[Sesame|ゴマ]]粉で濁したものが室町時代に用いられた。
今日では、清酒と濁酒（[[Nigori|どぶろく]]）の組を白酒・黒酒の代用とすることも多い。かつて、神酒は[[Shinto shrine|神社]]もしくは[[Ujigami|氏子]]が自家醸造していたが、現在は酒税法の規制があるため、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]のように[[Sake|清酒]]の醸造免許や、税務署からの[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の醸造許可を得ている神社も存在する。神酒の醸造目的などについては[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の項を参照。
[[Kumamoto Prefecture|熊本県]][[Kumamoto|熊本市]]の[[Kumamoto Castle|熊本城]]横にある[[Katō Shrine|加藤神社]]では地元伝統の赤酒がお神酒に使われている。
== Shinshu other than Japanese sake == [[Fukuoka Prefecture|福岡県]][[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|太宰府市]]の[[Dazaifu Tenmangū|太宰府天満宮]]では「飛梅」伝説にちなみ、[[Umeshu|梅酒]]（[[Nikka Whisky Distilling|ニッカ]]門司工場製造）がお神酒に使われている。
[[Grape|ブドウ]]栽培が盛んな地域では、[[Yamanashi Prefecture|山梨県]][[Fuefuki|笛吹市]]の[[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine|一宮浅間神社]]で[[Wine|ワイン]]がお神酒として奉納されたり、[[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]][[Habikino|羽曳野市]]の誉田八幡宮で[[Red wine|赤ワイン]]がお神酒として正月三が日に振舞われたりしている。
== その他 == なお江戸時代に[[Hinamatsuri|雛祭り]]で供えられる白酒の風習が生み出されたのは、白酒を供える風習が変化したものという説もある。
神酒を神棚に供える「御神酒徳利」は通常二本一組であることから、夫婦などが円満の譬えとされ、[[Rakugo|落語]]の演目にもある。御神酒徳利に挿す、[[Bambuseae|竹]]を割くなどして作った飾り物を神酒口（みきぐち）と言う<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>。
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.
== 海外 == 海外における宗教にまつわる神聖な酒については、古代[[Greek mythology|ギリシア神話]]は「[[Nectar (drink)|ネクタール]]」、[[Zoroastrianism|ゾロアスター教]]は「[[Haoma|ハオマ]]」、[[Hindu mythology|インド神話]]は「[[Soma (drink)|ソーマ]]」を参照。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* 灰持酒 - 黒酒が原型とされている * [[Amazake|甘酒]] * [[Sake|日本酒]]
== 外部リンク ==
* 山口県楠町商工会 岡崎八幡宮：全国でも珍しい白酒（しろき）の神社 - ウェイバックマシン（2016年3月4日アーカイブ分） [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|三方に乗せ献供された神酒]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink|shu]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
[Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen|Shinsei]], and is offered to the gods at festivals. The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit dwells, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. In some shrines, sake is served after the two-year visit to the shrine.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana|Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.
『[[Engishiki|延喜式]]』によれば、白酒は神田で採れた米で醸造した酒をそのまま濾したもの、黒酒は白酒に[[Clerodendrum trichotomum|常山木]]の根の焼灰を加えて黒く着色した酒（灰持酒）であると記載されている。後にこれに倣って[[Amazake|醴]]を、ゴマの肝臓機能強化を知ってか悪酔い止めにと黒[[Sesame|ゴマ]]粉で濁したものが室町時代に用いられた。
今日では、清酒と濁酒（[[Nigori|どぶろく]]）の組を白酒・黒酒の代用とすることも多い。かつて、神酒は[[Shinto shrine|神社]]もしくは[[Ujigami|氏子]]が自家醸造していたが、現在は酒税法の規制があるため、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]のように[[Sake|清酒]]の醸造免許や、税務署からの[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の醸造許可を得ている神社も存在する。神酒の醸造目的などについては[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の項を参照。
[[Kumamoto Prefecture|熊本県]][[Kumamoto|熊本市]]の[[Kumamoto Castle|熊本城]]横にある[[Katō Shrine|加藤神社]]では地元伝統の赤酒がお神酒に使われている。

[[Grape|ブドウ]]栽培が盛んな地域では、[[Yamanashi Prefecture|山梨県]][[Fuefuki|笛吹市]]の[[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine|一宮浅間神社]]で[[Wine|ワイン]]がお神酒として奉納されたり、[[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]][[Habikino|羽曳野市]]の誉田八幡宮で[[Red wine|赤ワイン]]がお神酒として正月三が日に振舞われたりしている。
== その他 == なお江戸時代に[[Hinamatsuri|雛祭り]]で供えられる白酒の風習が生み出されたのは、白酒を供える風習が変化したものという説もある。

In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.
== 海外 == 海外における宗教にまつわる神聖な酒については、古代[[Greek mythology|ギリシア神話]]は「[[Nectar (drink)|ネクタール]]」、[[Zoroastrianism|ゾロアスター教]]は「[[Haoma|ハオマ]]」、[[Hindu mythology|インド神話]]は「[[Soma (drink)|ソーマ]]」を参照。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* 灰持酒 - 黒酒が原型とされている * [[Amazake|甘酒]] * [[Sake|日本酒]]
== 外部リンク ==
* 山口県楠町商工会 岡崎八幡宮：全国でも珍しい白酒（しろき）の神社 - ウェイバックマシン（2016年3月4日アーカイブ分） [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|三方に乗せ献供された神酒]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink|shu]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].

These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.

== Shinshu other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu|Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of [[Tobi Ume]].
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]], and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].

In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.

今日では、清酒と濁酒（[[Nigori|どぶろく]]）の組を白酒・黒酒の代用とすることも多い。かつて、神酒は[[Shinto shrine|神社]]もしくは[[Ujigami|氏子]]が自家醸造していたが、現在は酒税法の規制があるため、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]のように[[Sake|清酒]]の醸造免許や、税務署からの[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の醸造許可を得ている神社も存在する。神酒の醸造目的などについては[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の項を参照。

At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu|Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of [[Tobi Ume]].
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]], and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|Bamboo]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>。
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.
『[[Engishiki|延喜式]]』によれば、白酒は神田で採れた米で醸造した酒をそのまま濾したもの、黒酒は白酒に[[Clerodendrum trichotomum|常山木]]の根の焼灰を加えて黒く着色した酒（灰持酒）であると記載されている。後にこれに倣って[[Amazake|醴]]を、ゴマの肝臓機能強化を知ってか悪酔い止めにと黒[[Sesame|ゴマ]]粉で濁したものが室町時代に用いられた。
今日では、清酒と濁酒（[[Nigori|どぶろく]]）の組を白酒・黒酒の代用とすることも多い。かつて、神酒は[[Shinto shrine|神社]]もしくは[[Ujigami|氏子]]が自家醸造していたが、現在は酒税法の規制があるため、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]のように[[Sake|清酒]]の醸造免許や、税務署からの[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の醸造許可を得ている神社も存在する。神酒の醸造目的などについては[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の項を参照。
[[Kumamoto Prefecture|熊本県]][[Kumamoto|熊本市]]の[[Kumamoto Castle|熊本城]]横にある[[Katō Shrine|加藤神社]]では地元伝統の赤酒がお神酒に使われている。
== Shinshu other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu|Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of [[Tobi Ume]].
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]], and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|Bamboo]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>。
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.
== Overseas == For sacred drinks associated with religions overseas, see "[[Nectar (drink)|Nectar]]" for ancient [[Greek mythology|Greek mythology]], "[[Haoma|Haoma]]" for [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]], and "[[Soma (drink)|Soma]]" for [[Hindu mythology|Indian mythology]], and [[Sacramental wine]] for [[Christianity]]
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* 灰持酒 - 黒酒が原型とされている * [[Amazake|甘酒]] * [[Sake|日本酒]]
== 外部リンク ==
* 山口県楠町商工会 岡崎八幡宮：全国でも珍しい白酒（しろき）の神社 - ウェイバックマシン（2016年3月4日アーカイブ分） [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|三方に乗せ献供された神酒]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink|shu]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
[Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen|Shinsei]], and is offered to the gods at festivals. The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit dwells, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. In some shrines, sake is served after the two-year visit to the shrine.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana|Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.
『[[Engishiki|延喜式]]』によれば、白酒は神田で採れた米で醸造した酒をそのまま濾したもの、黒酒は白酒に[[Clerodendrum trichotomum|常山木]]の根の焼灰を加えて黒く着色した酒（灰持酒）であると記載されている。後にこれに倣って[[Amazake|醴]]を、ゴマの肝臓機能強化を知ってか悪酔い止めにと黒[[Sesame|ゴマ]]粉で濁したものが室町時代に用いられた。
今日では、清酒と濁酒（[[Nigori|どぶろく]]）の組を白酒・黒酒の代用とすることも多い。かつて、神酒は[[Shinto shrine|神社]]もしくは[[Ujigami|氏子]]が自家醸造していたが、現在は酒税法の規制があるため、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]のように[[Sake|清酒]]の醸造免許や、税務署からの[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の醸造許可を得ている神社も存在する。神酒の醸造目的などについては[[Nigori|どぶろく]]の項を参照。
[[Kumamoto Prefecture|熊本県]][[Kumamoto|熊本市]]の[[Kumamoto Castle|熊本城]]横にある[[Katō Shrine|加藤神社]]では地元伝統の赤酒がお神酒に使われている。
== Shinshu other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu|Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of [[Tobi Ume]].
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]], and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|Bamboo]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>。
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.
== Overseas == For sacred drinks associated with religions overseas, see "[[Nectar (drink)|Nectar]]" for ancient [[Greek mythology|Greek mythology]], "[[Haoma|Haoma]]" for [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]], and "[[Soma (drink)|Soma]]" for [[Hindu mythology|Indian mythology]], and [[Sacramental wine]] for [[Christianity]]

== Related Items ==
* [[Ashimochi-shu]] - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake|Amazake]] * [[Sake|Japanese sake]]
== 外部リンク ==
* 山口県楠町商工会 岡崎八幡宮：全国でも珍しい白酒（しろき）の神社 - ウェイバックマシン（2016年3月4日アーカイブ分） [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|三方に乗せ献供された神酒]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink|shu]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
[Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen|Shinsei]], and is offered to the gods at festivals. The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit dwells, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. In some shrines, sake is served after the two-year visit to the shrine.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana|Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.
According to the [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum|Joyama tree]] roots to white sake and coloring it black. In the Muromachi period (1336-1573), [[Amazake]] was made by adding black [[Sesame]] powder to [[Ōzake]] to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the liver.
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori|Doburoku]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori|Doburoku]] from the tax office. Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori|Doburoku]], please refer to the [[Nigori|Doburoku]] section.
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Shinshu other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu|Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of [[Tobi Ume]].
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]], and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|Bamboo]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>。
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a soft drink. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.
== Overseas == For sacred drinks associated with religions overseas, see "[[Nectar (drink)|Nectar]]" for ancient [[Greek mythology|Greek mythology]], "[[Haoma|Haoma]]" for [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]], and "[[Soma (drink)|Soma]]" for [[Hindu mythology|Indian mythology]], and [[Sacramental wine]] for [[Christianity]]

revision.text:
[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods at festivals. The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit dwells, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. In some shrines, sake is served after the two-year visit to the shrine.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black. In the Muromachi period (1336-1573), [[Amazake]] was made by adding black [[Sesame]] powder to [[Ōzake]] to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the liver.
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods at festivals. The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit dwells, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. In some shrines, sake is served after the two-year visit to the shrine.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].

== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals. The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>. In the Muromachi period (1336-1573), [[Amazake]] was made by adding black [[Sesame]] powder to [[Ōzake]] to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the liver.
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.

== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the Honda Hachiman Shrine in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.<ref name="f10">[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>. Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.<ref name="f10">[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>

Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
日本では、古来より来期の豊穣を[[Prayer|祈願]]するため、どぶろくを作ってお供えする風習があり、現代でも40以上の神社で[[:ja:どぶろく祭|どぶろく祭]]等が行われている<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}</ref>。このような宗教的行事におけるどぶろくの製造は、濁酒の製造免許を受けて行われており、原則として[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の境内等の一定の敷地内から持ち出すことが禁じられている<ref name="SakeNo09" />。
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.

It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.<ref name="f10">[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>. Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}</ref>。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />。
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the Edo period (1603-1868) was created as a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
It is said that the custom of offering white sake at [[Hinamatsuri]] was created from the custom of offering white sake. It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae]] that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is also a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868) was a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of split bamboo or the like that are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.
It is also used in [[Rakugo]]. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>

Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>. Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}</ref>。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />。
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is also a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868) was a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.

* [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016) [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>

Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}</ref>。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />。
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.<ref name="f10">[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>

According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>. Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}</ref>。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />。
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>

The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>

revision.comment: MaitreyaVaruna moved page [[Omiki]] to [[Draft:Omiki]]: not ready for mainspace
revision.text:
[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.
Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.<ref name="f10">[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.
Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>. Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s. Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}</ref>。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />。
In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.
== Omiki other than Japanese sake ==
At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>
In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社}}</ref> and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is also a theory that the custom of offering white sake at the [[Hinamatsuri]] in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868) was a variation of the custom of offering white sake.
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） </ref>
In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Items ==
* {{Interlanguage link|link=Akumochizake|prefix=ja|foreign=灰持酒}} - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]]
== External link ==
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016) [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] '''Omiki''' (miki, shinsyu) is an [[alcoholic drink]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
[[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.<ref name="Kasulis2004">{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}}</ref> Sakes served to gods as offerings prior to drinking are called Omiki or Miki (お神酒, 神酒).<ref name="Bocking2005">{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}}</ref> People drink Omiki with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year.
== Overview == The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake, with "o" (mi) added to "sake". The word "miki" is a beautiful name for sake. There is a word for "comb" in [[Kojiki]], and a word for "ugus" in [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. These are connected to the word "kushi", which is due to the fact that the effects of sake were considered to be auspicious.

Some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].

Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].

Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]]. For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.

/* Overview */ commented out seemingly irrelevant part from Japanese Wikipedia
/* Overview */ added etymological info and added citations from Wiktionary https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92
