revision.text: {{AFC submission|d|mergeto|Religion and alcohol|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=AngusWOOF|declinets=20220301202633|ts=20220228041858}} {{AFC comment|1= This draft is a request to [[WP:SPINOFF|spin off]] an article. Proposals to spin out a topic from an article into another stand-alone article should be discussed at the talk page of the existing article, unless the draft satisfies a [[WP:SNG|special notability guide]]. If the draft satisfies a special notability guide, please identify the notability criterion with a [[WP:RS|reliable source]]. This does not mean that the draft should be spun off, and it does not mean that the draft should not be spun off. It does mean that discussion should be on the talk page of the existing article, [[Talk:{{{1}}}]]. (If this draft is resubmitted without discussion on the talk page of the existing article and does not satisfy a special notability guide, it may be Rejected or [[WP:MFD|nominated for deletion]].) [[User:AngusWOOF|AngusW🐶🐶F]] ([[User talk:AngusWOOF#top|bark]] • [[Special:Contributions/AngusWOOF|sniff]]) 20:26, 1 March 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto ritual offering of alcohol}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} [[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] {{ill|Omiki|ja|神酒}} {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} is an offering of [[sake]] or other [[alcoholic drink|alcoholic drinks]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}} [[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}} Sakes served to gods as offerings prior to drinking are called Omiki or Miki (お神酒, 神酒).{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}} People drink Omiki with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year. == Overview == The word Omiki is composed of three characters {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} the first {{Nihongo|2=お|3=O}} is an honorific prefix, the second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} is normally read as [[kami]], but in this compound and others it is read as mi, as it is in {{nihongo||[[wiktionary:巫女|巫女]]|'''[[miko]]'''}}, the third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as [[sake]] when alone, but in the kunyomi compound it is read as ki. The compound is very old and dates back to [[Old Japanese]]DaijirinDaijisenKokugo Dai Jiten Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10 The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=What does this mean? Is it some kind of initiation or a biyearly thing?|date=February 2022}} In addition, some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=What does this mean? Do they deliver it?|date=February 2022}} == Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}} The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}} According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black''Engishiki (''延喜式). Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].。 In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} == Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。 In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}} and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}} == Others == There is also a theory that the custom of white sake offered at the [[Hinamatsuri]] was created during the [[Edo period]] because the custom of offering white sake changed..{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=Offering white sake was a variation of offering white sake? Seems like there was a machine translation error somewhere|date=February 2022}} Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} == Related Items == * {{Interlanguage link|link=Akumochizake|prefix=ja|foreign=灰持酒}} - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] [[:Category:Rituals]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] == References == {{Reflist}} == External link == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016) [[:Category:Sake]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

(If this draft is resubmitted without discussion on the talk page of the existing article and does not satisfy a special notability guide, it may be Rejected or [[WP:MFD|nominated for deletion]].) [[User:AngusWOOF|AngusW🐶🐶F]] ([[User talk:AngusWOOF#top|bark]] • [[Special:Contributions/AngusWOOF|sniff]]) 20:26, 1 March 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto ritual offering of alcohol}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} [[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] {{ill|Omiki|ja|神酒}} {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} is an offering of [[sake]] or other [[alcoholic drink|alcoholic drinks]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}} [[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}} Sakes served to gods as offerings prior to drinking are called Omiki or Miki (お神酒, 神酒).{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}} People drink Omiki with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year. == Overview == The word Omiki is composed of three characters {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} the first {{Nihongo|2=お|3=O}} is an honorific prefix, the second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} is normally read as [[kami]], but in this compound and others it is read as mi, as it is in {{nihongo||[[wiktionary:巫女|巫女]]|'''[[miko]]'''}}, the third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as [[sake]] when alone, but in the kunyomi compound it is read as ki. The compound is very old and dates back to [[Old Japanese]]DaijirinDaijisenKokugo Dai Jiten Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10 The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=What does this mean? Is it some kind of initiation or a biyearly thing?|date=February 2022}} In addition, some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=What does this mean? Do they deliver it?|date=February 2022}} == Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}} The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}} According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black''Engishiki (''延喜式). Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].。 In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} == Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。 In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}} and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Honda Hachiman Shrine|ja|誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}} == Others == There is also a theory that the custom of white sake offered at the [[Hinamatsuri]] was created during the [[Edo period]] because the custom of offering white sake changed..{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=Offering white sake was a variation of offering white sake? Seems like there was a machine translation error somewhere|date=February 2022}} Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} == Related Items == * {{Interlanguage link|link=Akumochizake|prefix=ja|foreign=|Akumochizake|ja|灰持酒}} - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] [[:Category:Rituals]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] == References == {{Reflist}} == External link == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016) [[:Category:Sake]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

[[User:AngusWOOF|AngusW🐶🐶F]] ([[User talk:AngusWOOF#top|bark]] • [[Special:Contributions/AngusWOOF|sniff]]) 20:26, 1 March 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto ritual offering of alcohol}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} [[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on {{Interlanguage link|link=Mikata|prefix=ja|foreign=三方_(神道)}}]] {{ill|Omiki|ja|神酒}} {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} is an offering of [[sake]] or other [[alcoholic drink|alcoholic drinks]] offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}} [[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}} Sakes served to gods as offerings prior to drinking are called Omiki or Miki (お神酒, 神酒).{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}} People drink Omiki with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year. == Overview == The word Omiki is composed of three characters {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} the first {{Nihongo|2=お|3=O}} is an honorific prefix, the second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} is normally read as [[kami]], but in this compound and others it is read as mi, as it is in {{nihongo||[[wiktionary:巫女|巫女]]|'''[[miko]]'''}}, the third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as [[sake]] when alone, but in the kunyomi compound it is read as ki. The compound is very old and dates back to [[Old Japanese]]DaijirinDaijisenKokugo Dai Jiten Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during the rituals.[[Shinsen#国学院|日本の食とこころ]] P.10 The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}. There are also shrines where sake is served after worshipping for two years.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=What does this mean? Is it some kind of initiation or a biyearly thing?|date=February 2022}} In addition, some breweries offer new sake to the [[Kamidana]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=What does this mean? Do they deliver it?|date=February 2022}} == Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as Shiroki, Kuroki, Sumizake, and Nigorizake, and many different brewing methods. The "ki" in "baijiu" and "kuroki" is the old name for sake, and is also written "baijiu" and "kurokiu. Black liquor is also called "kuro-miki.{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}} The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}} According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black''Engishiki (''延喜式). Later, in the Muromachi period (1336-1573), sweet sake was made with [[Sesame|black sesame]] powder to prevent sickness, probably because sesame seeds strengthen the [[liver]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[doburoku]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} For more information on the purpose of brewing [[Nigori]], please refer to the [[Nigori]] section.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[pray|pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold {{ill|Doburoku Matsuri|ja|どぶろく祭}}, etc.{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21}}。As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[Shinto shrine]].。 In [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[Katō Shrine]] next to [[Kumamoto Castle]] in [[Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of red sake as its sacred wine.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} == Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。 In areas where [[Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[ Osaka Prefecture]],{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}} and [[Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{Interlanguage link|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Honda Hachiman Shrine|ja|誉田八幡宮}} in [[Habikino|Habikino City]].{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}} == Others == There is also a theory that the custom of white sake offered at the [[Hinamatsuri]] was created during the [[Edo period]] because the custom of offering white sake changed..{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}{{Clarification needed|reason=Offering white sake was a variation of offering white sake? Seems like there was a machine translation error somewhere|date=February 2022}} Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口]つがる工芸店（2018年1月22日閲覧） In [[Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the Amami region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "miki" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} == Related Items == * {{Interlanguage link|link=Akumochizake|prefix=ja|foreign=|Akumochizake|ja|灰持酒}} - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] == References == {{Reflist}} == External link == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016) [[:Category:Sake]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[:Category:Rituals]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

This does not mean that the draft should be spun off, and it does not mean that the draft should not be spun off. It does mean that discussion should be on the talk page of the existing article, [[Talk:1]]. revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Miki (Shinto)]][[en:Omiki]][[fr:Miki (bière)]][[ja:神酒]][[simple:Omiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3313722]] Wikidata name: miki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual offering of alcohol}} {{Full citations needed|date=March 2023}}
[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on a ''[[sanbo]]'' altar]] ''[[:en:Omiki|Omiki]]'' {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} is an offering of [[sake]] or other [[:en:Alcoholic drink|Alcoholic drink]]s offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref name="Bocking2005">{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
[[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.<ref name="Kasulis2004">{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}}</ref> People drink ''omiki'' with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year.
== Etymology == The word ''omiki'' is spelled using the three characters {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}}. The first {{Nihongo|2=お|3=o}} is an honorific prefix. The second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} refers to ''[[kami]]'', a kind of spirit or deity in Japanese spirituality. This is normally read as ''[[kami]]'', but in certain compounds it is read as ''mi'', as we also see in the derivation of the word {{nihongo|||[[miko]]|"shrine maiden"}}. The third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as ''[[sake]]'' when used alone, but in certain compounds of ancient derivation it is read as ''ki''. The compound ''omiki'' is very old and dates back to [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]].<ref name="DJR">Daijirin</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="DJS">Daijisen</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="KDJ">Kokugo Dai Jiten</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}} There is a word {{Nihongo||くし|kushi}} in the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} of 712, spelled using this same kanji {{lang|ja|}}, connected to the word {{Nihongo||奇し|kushi|"mystical"}} in reference to the effects of sake.<ref name="KDJ"/>
== Overview == Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during rituals.<ref>{{Cite book |author=國學院大學日本文化研究所 |title=日本の食とこころ - そのルーツと行方 |publisher=慶友社 |year=2003 |isbn=4-87449-233-9 |ref=国学院}}</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings.<ref>{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}</ref> Some shrines also serve sake after [[:en:Ninenmairi|Ninenmairi]].
Some breweries produce sake specifically to be put on [[Kamidana|kamidanas]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}}, {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}}, {{Nihongo|clear sake|清酒|Sumizake}}, and {{Nihongo|cloudy sake|濁酒|Nigorizake}}, and many different brewing methods. The "{{Nihongo|ki|酒}}" of {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}} and {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}} is the ancient name of sake, and is also written as "{{Nihongo|White Sake|白貴|Shiroki}}" and "{{Nihongo|Black Sake|黒貴|Kuroki}}". Black sake is also called {{Nihongo|2=黒御酒|3=Kuromiki}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref name="Bocking2005" />
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[:en:Clerodendrum trichotomum|Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black.<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[:en:Nigori|Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[:en:Pray|Pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold [[Doburoku Matsuri]], etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21|archive-date=2018-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304090922/http://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />
In [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[:en:Katō Shrine|Katō Shrine]] next to [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] as its sacred wine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=連載総集編 酒おもしろ小話@ |url=https://www-1ness-net.translate.goog/rensai/sake_01.html?_x_tr_sch=http&_x_tr_sl=ja&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=op,sc |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=www-1ness-net.translate.goog}}</ref>{{Verification needed|date=June 2022|reason=In reading it the source looks like it's saying this roughly but I don't understand it enough to be sure}}
== Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[:en:Umeshu|Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[:en:Nikka Whisky Distilling|Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
In areas where [[:en:Grape|Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[:en:Wine|Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[:en:Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)|Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}}</ref> and [[:en:Red wine|Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{ill|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Konda Hachimangū|ja|誉田八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|qq=|1=Konda Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631426}} in [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], at the shrine it is called {{Nihongo|[[:en:Hinomaru|Hinomaru]] Miki|日の丸神酒}} because the red wine pooled on a white plate appears similar to the Japanese flag.<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1867).{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[:en:Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口] つがる工芸店（2018th year 1月22日閲覧）</ref>
In [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami]] region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "[[:en:Miki (Okinawa)|Miki]]" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[:en:Soft drink|Soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「ミキ」を徹底研究してみた。→奥深さの沼にはまった。 「てみた。」１６ |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/article/entry-598076.html |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=琉球新報Style |language=ja}}</ref>
== Related Items == * [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] * [[Doburoku Matsuri]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[:en:Wayback Machine|Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016)

In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[:en:Pray|Pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold [[Doburoku Matsuri]], etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21|archive-date=2018-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304090922/http://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />
In [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[:en:Katō Shrine|Katō Shrine]] next to [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] as its sacred wine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=連載総集編 酒おもしろ小話@ |url=https://www-1ness-net.translate.goog/rensai/sake_01.html?_x_tr_sch=http&_x_tr_sl=ja&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=op,sc |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=www-1ness-net.translate.goog}}</ref>{{Verification needed|date=June 2022|reason=In reading it the source looks like it's saying this roughly but I don't understand it enough to be sure}}
== Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[:en:Umeshu|Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[:en:Nikka Whisky Distilling|Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
In areas where [[:en:Grape|Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[:en:Wine|Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[:en:Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)|Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}}</ref> and [[:en:Red wine|Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{ill|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Konda Hachimangū|ja|誉田八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|qq=|1=Konda Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631426}} in [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], at the shrine it is called {{Nihongo|[[:en:Hinomaru|Hinomaru]] Miki|日の丸神酒}} because the red wine pooled on a white plate appears similar to the Japanese flag.<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1867).{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[:en:Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口] つがる工芸店（2018th year 1月22日閲覧）</ref>
In [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami]] region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "[[:en:Miki (Okinawa)|Miki]]" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[:en:Soft drink|Soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「ミキ」を徹底研究してみた。→奥深さの沼にはまった。 「てみた。」１６ |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/article/entry-598076.html |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=琉球新報Style |language=ja}}</ref>
== Related Items == * [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] * [[Doburoku Matsuri]]

[[wiktionary:巫女|巫女]] [[wikt:酒#Japanese|酒]] [[en:Omiki]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Miki (Shinto)}} [[fr:Miki (bière)]] [[ja:神酒]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Omiki]] {{wikidata link|Q3313722}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Miki (Shinto)]][[en:Omiki]][[fr:Miki (bière)]][[ja:神酒]][[simple:Omiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3313722]] Wikidata name: miki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual offering of alcohol}} {{Full citations needed|date=March 2023}}
[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on a ''[[sanbo]]'' altar]] ''[[:en:Omiki|Omiki]]'' {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} is an offering of [[sake]] or other [[:en:Alcoholic drink|Alcoholic drink]]s offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref name="Bocking2005">{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
[[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.<ref name="Kasulis2004">{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}}</ref> People drink ''omiki'' with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year.
== Etymology == The word ''omiki'' is spelled using the three characters {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}}. The first {{Nihongo|2=お|3=o}} is an honorific prefix. The second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} refers to ''[[kami]]'', a kind of spirit or deity in Japanese spirituality. This is normally read as ''[[kami]]'', but in certain compounds it is read as ''mi'', as we also see in the derivation of the word {{nihongo|||[[miko]]|"shrine maiden"}}. The third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as ''[[sake]]'' when used alone, but in certain compounds of ancient derivation it is read as ''ki''. The compound ''omiki'' is very old and dates back to [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]].<ref name="DJR">Daijirin</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="DJS">Daijisen</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="KDJ">Kokugo Dai Jiten</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}} There is a word {{Nihongo||くし|kushi}} in the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} of 712, spelled using this same kanji {{lang|ja|}}, connected to the word {{Nihongo||奇し|kushi|"mystical"}} in reference to the effects of sake.<ref name="KDJ"/>
== Overview == Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during rituals.<ref>{{Cite book |author=國學院大學日本文化研究所 |title=日本の食とこころ - そのルーツと行方 |publisher=慶友社 |year=2003 |isbn=4-87449-233-9 |ref=国学院}}</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings.<ref>{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}</ref> Some shrines also serve sake after [[:en:Ninenmairi|Ninenmairi]].
Some breweries produce sake specifically to be put on [[Kamidana|kamidanas]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}}, {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}}, {{Nihongo|clear sake|清酒|Sumizake}}, and {{Nihongo|cloudy sake|濁酒|Nigorizake}}, and many different brewing methods. The "{{Nihongo|ki|酒}}" of {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}} and {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}} is the ancient name of sake, and is also written as "{{Nihongo|White Sake|白貴|Shiroki}}" and "{{Nihongo|Black Sake|黒貴|Kuroki}}". Black sake is also called {{Nihongo|2=黒御酒|3=Kuromiki}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref name="Bocking2005" />
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[:en:Clerodendrum trichotomum|Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black.<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[:en:Nigori|Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[:en:Pray|Pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold [[Doburoku Matsuri]], etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21|archive-date=2018-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304090922/http://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />
In [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[:en:Katō Shrine|Katō Shrine]] next to [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] as its sacred wine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=連載総集編 酒おもしろ小話@ |url=https://www-1ness-net.translate.goog/rensai/sake_01.html?_x_tr_sch=http&_x_tr_sl=ja&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=op,sc |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=www-1ness-net.translate.goog}}</ref>{{Verification needed|date=June 2022|reason=In reading it the source looks like it's saying this roughly but I don't understand it enough to be sure}}

In areas where [[:en:Grape|Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[:en:Wine|Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[:en:Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)|Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}}</ref> and [[:en:Red wine|Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{ill|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Konda Hachimangū|ja|誉田八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|qq=|1=Konda Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631426}} in [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], at the shrine it is called {{Nihongo|[[:en:Hinomaru|Hinomaru]] Miki|日の丸神酒}} because the red wine pooled on a white plate appears similar to the Japanese flag.<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1867).{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[:en:Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口] つがる工芸店（2018th year 1月22日閲覧）</ref>
In [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami]] region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "[[:en:Miki (Okinawa)|Miki]]" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[:en:Soft drink|Soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「ミキ」を徹底研究してみた。→奥深さの沼にはまった。 「てみた。」１６ |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/article/entry-598076.html |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=琉球新報Style |language=ja}}</ref>

== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}}, {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}}, {{Nihongo|clear sake|清酒|Sumizake}}, and {{Nihongo|cloudy sake|濁酒|Nigorizake}}, and many different brewing methods. The "{{Nihongo|ki|酒}}" of {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}} and {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}} is the ancient name of sake, and is also written as "{{Nihongo|White Sake|白貴|Shiroki}}" and "{{Nihongo|Black Sake|黒貴|Kuroki}}". Black sake is also called {{Nihongo|2=黒御酒|3=Kuromiki}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref name="Bocking2005" />
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[:en:Clerodendrum trichotomum|Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black.<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[:en:Nigori|Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[:en:Pray|Pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold [[Doburoku Matsuri]], etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21|archive-date=2018-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304090922/http://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />
In [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[:en:Katō Shrine|Katō Shrine]] next to [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] as its sacred wine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=連載総集編 酒おもしろ小話@ |url=https://www-1ness-net.translate.goog/rensai/sake_01.html?_x_tr_sch=http&_x_tr_sl=ja&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=op,sc |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=www-1ness-net.translate.goog}}</ref>{{Verification needed|date=June 2022|reason=In reading it the source looks like it's saying this roughly but I don't understand it enough to be sure}}
== Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[:en:Umeshu|Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[:en:Nikka Whisky Distilling|Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
In areas where [[:en:Grape|Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[:en:Wine|Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[:en:Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)|Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}}</ref> and [[:en:Red wine|Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{ill|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Konda Hachimangū|ja|誉田八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|1=Konda Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631426}} in [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], at the shrine it is called {{Nihongo|[[:en:Hinomaru|Hinomaru]] Miki|日の丸神酒}} because the red wine pooled on a white plate appears similar to the Japanese flag.<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1867).{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[:en:Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口] つがる工芸店（2018th year 1月22日閲覧）</ref>
In [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami]] region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "[[:en:Miki (Okinawa)|Miki]]" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[:en:Soft drink|Soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「ミキ」を徹底研究してみた。→奥深さの沼にはまった。 「てみた。」１６ |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/article/entry-598076.html |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=琉球新報Style |language=ja}}</ref>
== Related Items == * [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] * [[Doburoku Matsuri]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[:en:Wayback Machine|Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016)

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Miki (Shinto)}} [[fr:Miki (bière)]] [[ja:神酒]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Omiki]] {{wikidata link|Q3313722}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Miki (Shinto)]][[en:Omiki]][[fr:Miki (bière)]][[ja:神酒]][[simple:Omiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3313722]] Wikidata name: miki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual offering of alcohol}} {{Full citations needed|date=March 2023}}
[[File:Shinsen_002.jpg|thumb|271x271px|Sake offered on a ''[[sanbo]]'' altar]] ''[[:en:Omiki|Omiki]]'' {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}} is an offering of [[sake]] or other [[:en:Alcoholic drink|Alcoholic drink]]s offered to [[Kami|god]]s in Japanese [[Shinto]].<ref name="Bocking2005">{{cite book |author=Brian Bocking |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT175 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=30 September 2005 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-79738-6 |page=175}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=字通|first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,普及版|title=神酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92-537696|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=神酒(おみき)とは何？ Weblio辞書|url=https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E7%A5%9E%E9%85%92|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.weblio.jp}}</ref>
[[Sake]] is often consumed as part of [[Shinto]] purification rituals.<ref name="Kasulis2004">{{cite book |author=Thomas P. Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}}</ref> People drink ''omiki'' with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year.
== Etymology == The word ''omiki'' is spelled using the three characters {{Nihongo|2=お神酒}}. The first {{Nihongo|2=お|3=o}} is an honorific prefix. The second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} refers to ''[[kami]]'', a kind of spirit or deity in Japanese spirituality. This is normally read as ''[[kami]]'', but in certain compounds it is read as ''mi'', as we also see in the derivation of the word {{nihongo|||[[miko]]|"shrine maiden"}}. The third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as ''[[sake]]'' when used alone, but in certain compounds of ancient derivation it is read as ''ki''. The compound ''omiki'' is very old and dates back to [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]].<ref name="DJR">Daijirin</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="DJS">Daijisen</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="KDJ">Kokugo Dai Jiten</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}} There is a word {{Nihongo||くし|kushi}} in the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} of 712, spelled using this same kanji {{lang|ja|}}, connected to the word {{Nihongo||奇し|kushi|"mystical"}} in reference to the effects of sake.<ref name="KDJ"/>
== Overview == Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during rituals.<ref>{{Cite book |author=國學院大學日本文化研究所 |title=日本の食とこころ - そのルーツと行方 |publisher=慶友社 |year=2003 |isbn=4-87449-233-9 |ref=国学院}}</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings.<ref>{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}</ref> Some shrines also serve sake after [[:en:Ninenmairi|Ninenmairi]].
Some breweries produce sake specifically to be put on [[Kamidana|kamidanas]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}}, {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}}, {{Nihongo|clear sake|清酒|Sumizake}}, and {{Nihongo|cloudy sake|濁酒|Nigorizake}}, and many different brewing methods. The "{{Nihongo|ki|酒}}" of {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}} and {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}} is the ancient name of sake, and is also written as "{{Nihongo|White Sake|白貴|Shiroki}}" and "{{Nihongo|Black Sake|黒貴|Kuroki}}". Black sake is also called {{Nihongo|2=黒御酒|3=Kuromiki}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref name="Bocking2005" />
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[:en:Clerodendrum trichotomum|Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black.<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[:en:Nigori|Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[:en:Pray|Pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold [[Doburoku Matsuri]], etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21|archive-date=2018-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304090922/http://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />
In [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[:en:Katō Shrine|Katō Shrine]] next to [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] as its sacred wine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=連載総集編 酒おもしろ小話@ |url=https://www-1ness-net.translate.goog/rensai/sake_01.html?_x_tr_sch=http&_x_tr_sl=ja&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=op,sc |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=www-1ness-net.translate.goog}}</ref>{{Verification needed|date=June 2022|reason=In reading it the source looks like it's saying this roughly but I don't understand it enough to be sure}}
== Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[:en:Umeshu|Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[:en:Nikka Whisky Distilling|Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
In areas where [[:en:Grape|Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[:en:Wine|Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[:en:Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)|Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}}</ref> and [[:en:Red wine|Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{ill|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Konda Hachimangū|ja|誉田八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|1=Konda Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631426}} in [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], at the shrine it is called {{Nihongo|[[:en:Hinomaru|Hinomaru]] Miki|日の丸神酒}} because the red wine pooled on a white plate appears similar to the Japanese flag.<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1867).{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[:en:Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口] つがる工芸店（2018th year 1月22日閲覧）</ref>
In [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami]] region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "[[:en:Miki (Okinawa)|Miki]]" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[:en:Soft drink|Soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「ミキ」を徹底研究してみた。→奥深さの沼にはまった。 「てみた。」１６ |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/article/entry-598076.html |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=琉球新報Style |language=ja}}</ref>

Kasulis |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yQPWNilhjPcC&pg=PT70 |title=Shinto |date=August 2004 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6430-9 |page=70}}</ref> People drink ''omiki'' with gods to communicate with them and to solicit rich harvests the following year.

The second character {{Nihongo|2=神}} refers to ''[[kami]]'', a kind of spirit or deity in Japanese spirituality. This is normally read as ''[[kami]]'', but in certain compounds it is read as ''mi'', as we also see in the derivation of the word {{nihongo|||[[miko]]|"shrine maiden"}}. The third character {{Nihongo|2=酒}} is read as ''[[sake]]'' when used alone, but in certain compounds of ancient derivation it is read as ''ki''. The compound ''omiki'' is very old and dates back to [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]].<ref name="DJR">Daijirin</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="DJS">Daijisen</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}<ref name="KDJ">Kokugo Dai Jiten</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}} There is a word {{Nihongo||くし|kushi}} in the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} of 712, spelled using this same kanji {{lang|ja|}}, connected to the word {{Nihongo||奇し|kushi|"mystical"}} in reference to the effects of sake.<ref name="KDJ"/>
== Overview == Sake is an essential part of [[Shinsen]], and is offered to the gods during rituals.<ref>{{Cite book |author=國學院大學日本文化研究所 |title=日本の食とこころ - そのルーツと行方 |publisher=慶友社 |year=2003 |isbn=4-87449-233-9 |ref=国学院}}</ref> The meaning of this ceremony is to receive the sake that has been offered to the gods and in which the spirit resides, and to eat and drink the same food as the gods as well as other food offerings.<ref>{{Cite book |last=editor. |first=沼部春友, 1938- author, editor. 茂木貞純, 1951- author |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/705869854 |title=新神社祭式行事作法教本 |date=2011 |publisher=戎光祥出版 |isbn=978-4-86403-033-5 |pages=260–266 |language=Japanese |trans-title=Shinjinja Matsuri Style Practice Manual |oclc=705869854}}</ref> Some shrines also serve sake after [[:en:Ninenmairi|Ninenmairi]].
Some breweries produce sake specifically to be put on [[Kamidana|kamidanas]].
== Types and brewing methods == There are many different types of sake, such as {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}}, {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}}, {{Nihongo|clear sake|清酒|Sumizake}}, and {{Nihongo|cloudy sake|濁酒|Nigorizake}}, and many different brewing methods. The "{{Nihongo|ki|酒}}" of {{Nihongo|White Sake|白酒|Shiroki}} and {{Nihongo|black sake|黒酒|Kuroki}} is the ancient name of sake, and is also written as "{{Nihongo|White Sake|白貴|Shiroki}}" and "{{Nihongo|Black Sake|黒貴|Kuroki}}". Black sake is also called {{Nihongo|2=黒御酒|3=Kuromiki}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典|first=精選版|title=黒御酒とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E5%BE%A1%E9%85%92-253648|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref name="Bocking2005" />
The origin of amazake dates back to the [[Kofun period]] , and there is a description in " [[Nihon Shoki]] " about Amazake, which is said to be the origin of amazake.<ref>{{Cite web|title=甘酒｜森永製菓|url=https://www.morinaga.co.jp/amazake/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=www.morinaga.co.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
According to the [[Engishiki]], white sake is brewed from rice grown in Kanda and strained as it is, while black sake is made by adding burned ashes of [[:en:Clerodendrum trichotomum|Clerodendrum trichotomum]] roots to white sake and coloring it black.<ref>''Engishiki (''延喜式)</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Today, a combination of clear sake and cloudy sake ([[:en:Nigori|Nigori]]) is often used as a substitute for white and black sake. In the past, sacred sake was home-brewed by [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s or [[Ujigami]]s.{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}} Nowadays, however, due to restrictions in the Liquor Tax Law, [[Ise Grand Shrine]]s are required to obtain a license to brew [[Sake]]s and a [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]] from the tax office. [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which has a license to brew [[sake]] and a permit from the tax office to brew [[:en:Nigori|Nigori]].{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
In Japan, it has been customary since ancient times to make doburoku and offer it to the gods to [[:en:Pray|Pray]] for a good harvest in the coming year, and even today, more than 40 shrines hold [[Doburoku Matsuri]], etc.<ref name="SakeNo09">{{Cite web|title=酒類総合研究所情報誌「お酒のはなし」第9号|url=https://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|publisher=[[酒類総合研究所]]|accessdate=2019-01-21|archive-date=2018-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304090922/http://www.nrib.go.jp/sake/pdf/SakeNo09.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> As a rule, it is forbidden to take doburoku out of the premises of a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="SakeNo09" />
In [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], the [[:en:Katō Shrine|Katō Shrine]] next to [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] uses the local tradition of [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] as its sacred wine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=連載総集編 酒おもしろ小話@ |url=https://www-1ness-net.translate.goog/rensai/sake_01.html?_x_tr_sch=http&_x_tr_sl=ja&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=op,sc |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=www-1ness-net.translate.goog}}</ref>{{Verification needed|date=June 2022|reason=In reading it the source looks like it's saying this roughly but I don't understand it enough to be sure}}
== Omiki other than Japanese sake == At [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu City]], [[:en:Umeshu|Umeshu]] Plum Wine (produced by [[:en:Nikka Whisky Distilling|Nikka Whisky Distilling]]'s Moji factory) is used as a sacred wine in honor of the legend of Tobi Ume.<ref>[[ニッカウヰスキー]]（旧[[協和発酵キリン|協和発酵]]→[[アサヒ協和酒類製造]]）門司工場製。梅の実は境内で収穫し御祓いを受けたものを使い、これを焼酎のラインで梅酒として製造する。</ref>{{Full citation needed|date=March 2023}}
In areas where [[:en:Grape|Grape]] cultivation is popular, [[:en:Wine|Wine]] is dedicated as a sacred wine at the [[:en:Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)|Ichinomiya Asama Shrine]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki City]], and [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=淺間神社|date=2016-10-04|title=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社: 山梨県立博物館企画展に浅間神社の奉納ワイン棚が登場します！|url=https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|access-date=2021-12-01|website=甲斐国一宮 浅間神社|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201021080204/https://asama-jinja.blogspot.com/2016/10/blog-post.html|archive-date=2020-10-21}}</ref> and [[:en:Red wine|Red wine]] is served as a sacred wine on the third day of the New Year at the {{ill|link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|prefix=ja|foreign=誉田八幡宮|Konda Hachimangū|ja|誉田八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/link=Honda Hachiman Shrine|1=Konda Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631426}} in [[:en:Habikino|Habikino City]], at the shrine it is called {{Nihongo|[[:en:Hinomaru|Hinomaru]] Miki|日の丸神酒}} because the red wine pooled on a white plate appears similar to the Japanese flag.<ref>{{Cite web|last=author|date=2019-01-02|title=誉田八幡宮の初詣で「日の丸神酒」を頂いて来ました。～羽曳野市 アクセス～|url=https://travel-minakawa.com/2019/01/02/hinomarumiki/|access-date=2022-02-26|website=南河内に何がある？｜南河内の観光スポットを紹介|language=ja}}</ref>
== Others == There is a theory that the custom of offering white sake at [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hina Matsuri]] was created in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1867).{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}
Since the sake is usually offered to the altar in pairs, it is used as a metaphor for the happiness of married couples, and is also used in [[rakugo]] storytelling. The ornaments made of [[:en:Bambuseae|split bamboo]] and are inserted into the sake tokuri are called mikiguchi.<ref>[http://www.komakino.jp/tugaru/mikiguti.html 神酒口] つがる工芸店（2018th year 1月22日閲覧）</ref>
In [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], which is part of the former [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]], and the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami]] region of Kagoshima Prefecture, a unique rice-based beverage called "[[:en:Miki (Okinawa)|Miki]]" has been handed down and is also sold commercially as a [[:en:Soft drink|Soft drink]]. It is made by adding sugar to crushed rice and fermenting it naturally.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「ミキ」を徹底研究してみた。→奥深さの沼にはまった。 「てみた。」１６ |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/article/entry-598076.html |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=琉球新報Style |language=ja}}</ref>
== Related Items == * [[:en:Akumochizake|Akumochizake]] - believed to be the original black liquor * [[Amazake]] * [[Sake]] * [[Doburoku Matsuri]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304105530/http://www.stellar.meon.ne.jp/~kusunokichoshokokai/seinen/okazaki/okazakihatimannguu.htm Okazaki Hachimangu Shrine, Kusunoki-cho Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Yamaguchi Prefecture: a rare shrine for Shiroki (white sake) in Japan] - [[:en:Wayback Machine|Wayback Machine]] (archived March 4, 2016)
